file_name: Bozkurt - 2022 - Skin lesion classification on dermatoscopic images using effective data augmentation and pre-trained.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a pre-trained deep learning approach, specifically a hybrid Inception-ResNet-v2 model, for skin lesion classification on dermatoscopic images. It employs data augmentation (affine transformation) to improve classification accuracy, achieving 95.09% on the augmented dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid Inception-ResNet-v2 model for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods including data augmentation (affine transformation), experimental setup (Adam optimizer, 100 epochs), and reports new quantitative results (95.09% accuracy on augmented dataset), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin lesion classification on dermatoscopic images using effective data augmentation and pre-trained deep learning approach
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of limited data in skin cancer classification by proposing an effective data augmentation and pre-trained deep learning approach. The authors utilized the public MNIST HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven classes of pigmented skin lesions. To overcome data scarcity, affine transformation was applied to augment the training set, increasing the number of available images. The proposed hybrid Inception-ResNet-v2 model was evaluated on both the original and augmented datasets to assess the impact of augmentation on classification accuracy. The model achieved a highest reported accuracy of 95.09% on the augmented dataset, significantly outperforming its 83.59% accuracy on the original dataset. These results demonstrate that effective data augmentation combined with pre-trained deep learning can improve diagnostic performance for skin lesion classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as 'Benign or malignant' in the HAM10000 dataset; 327 original images, 3,269 augmented images.
Classified as 'Malignant' in the HAM10000 dataset; 514 original images, 5,135 augmented images.
Classified as 'Benign' in the HAM10000 dataset; 1,099 original images, 10,987 augmented images.
Classified as 'Benign' in the HAM10000 dataset; 115 original images, 1,150 augmented images.
Classified as 'Malignant' in the HAM10000 dataset; 1,113 original images, 11,122 augmented images.
Classified as 'Benign' in the HAM10000 dataset; 6,705 original images, 6,705 augmented images.
Classified as 'Benign or malignant' in the HAM10000 dataset; 142 original images, 1,419 augmented images.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct classes of pigmented skin lesions (Actinic keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a hybrid Inception-ResNet-v2 model for skin lesion classification on dermatoscopic images. It uses the HAM10000 dataset with seven classes. The task is explicitly defined as classification, with performance measured by accuracy (95.09% on augmented dataset). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion types) rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: MNIST HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing multi-source dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions. The original dataset consists of 10,015 images with a size of 600x450 pixels in RGB format, which were rescaled to 224x224 pixels for the model.

use.training: Used as the original dataset for training the Inception-ResNet-v2 model. Additionally, an augmented version of this dataset (39,787 images) was created using affine transformation and used for training.
use.other: The original dataset was also used to evaluate the baseline performance of the model before augmentation.

statistical_methods_used.method: Repeated runs on different training and test sets
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate average accuracy values and form boxplots showing min-max and average values for performance comparison of different pre-trained models.
To visualize the distribution of accuracy results (min, max, average) across multiple runs for comparing different deep learning models.
To calculate and report per-class and average Precision, Recall, and F1-Score metrics for model evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy metrics and boxplots showing min-max and average values from repeated runs, but no statistical significance testing was employed.

MNIST HAM10000 (Original Dataset).description: Original dataset with 10,015 images, split into 70% training, 10% validation, and 20% testing.
MNIST HAM10000 (Original Dataset).notes: The Inception-ResNet-v2 model achieved the highest accuracy among the compared pre-trained models on the original dataset. Results are based on 10 runs on different training and test sets.

MNIST HAM10000 (Augmented Dataset).description: Augmented dataset with 39,787 images created using affine transformation, split into 70% training, 10% validation, and 20% testing.
MNIST HAM10000 (Augmented Dataset).notes: The Inception-ResNet-v2 model achieved the highest accuracy (95.09%) among all compared methods on the augmented dataset. The boxplot analysis showed min-max and average values from 10 runs. The model also had the lowest execution time (1h 50min 04s) among the compared pre-trained models.

preprocessing_techniques: Rescaling images from original size 600x450 pixels to 224x224 pixels for the model input
Data augmentation via affine transformation to increase the number of training images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Each image in each class (except Melanocytic nevi) was randomly rotated nine times with angles between 0 and 360 degrees (specifically noted as 0 to 90 degrees in the text description of Fig 3). This increased the number of images per class tenfold (original + 9 augmented). The Melanocytic nevi class was not augmented.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid Inception-ResNet-v2 model combined with affine transformation data augmentation achieved a classification accuracy of 95.09% on the augmented HAM10000 dataset, significantly outperforming its 83.59% accuracy on the original dataset.
Data augmentation via affine transformation effectively addresses the challenge of limited real-world data for skin cancer classification, leading to improved diagnostic performance.

The Inception-ResNet-v2 model outperformed other tested pre-trained deep learning models (VGG16, VGG19, SqueezeNet, LeNet-5, AlexNet, and a custom Deep CNN) in terms of accuracy on the augmented dataset.
The Inception-ResNet-v2 model demonstrated the lowest execution time (1 hour 50 minutes 4 seconds) among the compared pre-trained models on the augmented dataset.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the scarcity of real image data for deadly diseases like melanoma and plans future work on cost-sensitive models for minority class misclassification and effective dimension reduction.
Future research will involve comparing other deep learning models and hybrid methods on augmented datasets using additional affine transformation techniques.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant limitation regarding the scarcity of real-world image data for deadly diseases like melanoma, which hinders the application of machine learning techniques.
The dataset (HAM10000) is imbalanced, with 'Melanocytic nevi' (nv) comprising 6,705 images while 'Dermatofibroma' (df) has only 115, potentially biasing model performance towards majority classes.

Data augmentation was performed exclusively on the training set using affine transformation (random rotation), increasing the dataset size from 10,015 to 39,787 images, but the 'nv' class was not augmented.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons rely on accuracy metrics and boxplots from 10 repeated runs.
The author declares no conflict of interests, and data availability is stated as not applicable as no new datasets were generated or analyzed beyond the public HAM10000 dataset.

The study focuses on improving classification accuracy through data augmentation and pre-trained models but does not address external validation on independent datasets or clinical deployment challenges.
